hero.title: Explainable multimodal emotion analysis from children's drawings
hero.subtitle: This prototype evaluates children's drawings with an EfficientNet-B0 based architecture trained on the Qwen-selected dataset. It classifies drawings into four emotion categories — Happy, Sad, Angry, and Fear — and provides explainable decision-support outputs to assist expert review.
highlights: A 4-class emotion classifier (Happy / Sad / Angry / Fear) trained on 2,696 drawings (Qwen-selected + SigLIP-filtered)
Explainable outputs with Grad-CAM and clinical feature scoring
A research prototype delivered through a FastAPI service and React interface

home.panelTitle: The report distilled into one screen
home.panelSubtitle: The system processes the drawing image together with optional text to predict emotion, while gender classification is handled as an auxiliary task to strengthen shared representation learning.
panelFlow: The drawing is accepted as input and prepared at 224x224 resolution.
If text is available, it is tokenized with BERT up to a maximum length of 128 tokens.
EfficientNet-B0 and BERTurk outputs are fused, then emotion and auxiliary gender heads are evaluated.
home.quickCardOneText: EfficientNet-B0 is used for the visual stream, while dbmdz/bert-base-turkish-cased powers the text stream.
home.quickCardTwoText: Grad-CAM heatmaps and token importance scores make model decisions more transparent.

stats.label: Total samples
stats.note: 1,408 Qwen-selected drawings plus 1,712 SigLIP-filtered drawings (428 per class).

stats.label: Train / val / test
stats.note: Train expanded with SigLIP data; val and test remain the original 212 Qwen-selected samples each.

stats.label: Emotion categories
stats.note: Happy, Sad, Angry, Fear — balanced at ~673 training samples each.

stats.label: Test accuracy
stats.note: EfficientNet-B0 backbone, best checkpoint at epoch 7 (val macro F1 = 0.394).

home.workflowIntro: The home page summarizes the experimental pipeline from the report as a clear product flow.

workflowSteps.title: Data preparation
workflowSteps.body: 1,408 drawings from dataset_qwen_selected are split 70/15/15 into train, validation, and test sets using stratified sampling, producing a manifest CSV for reproducible training.

workflowSteps.title: Image-based inference
workflowSteps.body: Visual features are extracted with EfficientNet-B0 and combined with a clinical feature MLP to feed the 4-class emotion head (Happy / Sad / Angry / Fear).

workflowSteps.title: Explanation and delivery
workflowSteps.body: Outputs are presented together with per-class confidence scores and Grad-CAM heatmaps highlighting the drawing regions most influential in the prediction.

home.modulesTitle: Model, service, and interface were designed together
home.modulesIntro: According to the report, the project did not stop at a training run; it also produced a reproducible experimentation layer and a user-facing prototype.

modules.title: Data and experiment layer
modules.body: The manifest builder (build_manifest_qwen.py) performs stratified splitting and produces a reproducible CSV manifest from dataset_qwen_selected.

modules.title: Image classification core
modules.body: EfficientNet-B0 backbone feeds a projection head and clinical MLP, whose fused output is classified into 4 emotion categories.

modules.title: Explainability module
modules.body: Visual focus regions are shown with Grad-CAM, making the model's attention on specific drawing areas transparent to the expert.

modules.title: Service and presentation
modules.body: The FastAPI inference service and React interface turn the research output into an interactive prototype.

home.outcomesIntro: The project outcome is not only a trained model; it is an end-to-end experimentation and presentation chain.
outcomes: Reproducible scripts for training, evaluation, and reporting
Prototype tools for single-sample prediction and explanation generation
A local service layer built around the FastAPI /api/predict endpoint
A React-based front end that exposes the workflow to expert users
home.ethicsText: As stated in the report, this system is not a clinical diagnosis tool. It should be used as an explainable decision-support prototype that assists expert judgment.

analysis.title: Run Analysis
analysis.subtitle: Upload a drawing to generate an inference report.
analysis.drop: Drop image here or click to upload
analysis.disclaimer: This tool is a prototype intended for academic research. Its outputs are probabilistic and must not be interpreted as a clinical diagnosis. Professional assessment should always involve qualified experts.
analysis.textPlaceholder: Enter any available description about the drawing...
analysis.noExplanation: No explanation was returned by the backend for this result.
analysis.backendHint: Make sure the Python API server is running: uvicorn api_server:app --port 8000

about.objectiveText: This project developed an explainable artificial intelligence system capable of detecting emotional states from children's drawings. The system classifies drawings into four emotion categories — Happy, Sad, Angry, and Fear — using an EfficientNet-B0 backbone trained on the Qwen-selected dataset, and provides Grad-CAM based visual explanations to support expert review.

facts.label: Project title
facts.value: Emotion State Analysis with Deep Learning from Children's Drawings

facts.label: Institution
facts.value: Nigde Omer Halisdemir University, Computer Engineering

goals: Build a workable data structure that combines drawing images with paired text
Develop a deep learning model that performs emotion classification
Deliver a prototype system that presents the results to users
about.dataIntro: The project used the Qwen-selected dataset (dataset_qwen_selected) as the primary source, expanded with SigLIP-filtered drawings (Emotion_SigLIP_4Class_Filtered_045). Images were curated into four balanced emotion categories for training.
dataPoints: A total of 3,120 samples: 1,408 Qwen-selected + 1,712 SigLIP-filtered (428 per class).
Qwen-selected class distribution: Angry 357, Happy 350, Sad 350, Fear 351 — near-perfectly balanced.
Train set: 2,696 samples (~673 per class); val: 212; test: 212 (Qwen originals preserved).
Emotion classes: Happy, Sad, Angry, Fear — a 4-class classification task.
Image-only pipeline; SigLIP data added with folder labels (confidence ≥ 0.45 pre-filtered).
about.methodologyIntro: The report describes the multimodal and multi-task design across four main areas.

methodologyCards.title: General architecture
items: EfficientNet-B0 is used as the visual backbone (pretrained on ImageNet).
A projection MLP maps the 1280-dim backbone output to 256 dims.
A clinical feature MLP handles optional 18-dim feature vectors.
A fusion head outputs 4-class logits (Happy / Sad / Angry / Fear).

methodologyCards.title: Pre-processing
items: Training augmentation: random rotation ±10°, color jitter (brightness/contrast).
Images are normalized with ImageNet statistics.
Weighted random sampling is used to handle any remaining class imbalance.

methodologyCards.title: Training setup
items: Up to 60 epochs, batch size 32, learning rate 8e-5, early stopping patience 12.
AdamW optimizer with weight decay 2e-4; cosine annealing LR schedule.
Weighted cross-entropy loss (label smoothing 0.10); random seed fixed at 42.
Best checkpoint selected by validation macro F1 (best at epoch 7, val F1 = 0.394).

methodologyCards.title: Explainability
items: Grad-CAM highlights the visual regions of the drawing that drive the model decision.
Per-class probability bars show the confidence across all 4 emotion categories.
The system returns not only a label but also the rationale behind the decision.

about.resultsIntro: 4-class emotion classification on the Qwen-selected test set (212 samples). Best checkpoint from epoch 7 (expanded training) with validation macro F1 = 0.394.
about.emotionResultsText: Test set results across Happy, Sad, Angry, Fear. Happy achieved the highest F1 score (0.55), followed by Fear (0.46).
about.genderResultsText: Individual class F1 scores on the 212-sample test set. Each class has approximately 52–54 test examples.
deliverables: A trained 4-class emotion model (best.pt at epoch 7, val macro F1 = 0.394) with reproducible training scripts
Manifest builder, SigLIP data expansion pipeline, and stratified split pipeline for dataset_qwen_selected
A FastAPI service layer that returns predictions, confidence scores, explanations, and Grad-CAM heatmaps
A React-based web interface and desktop prototype approach for user-facing interaction
limitations: The dataset is relatively small (1,408 samples), which limits generalization to unseen drawings.
Test accuracy of 43.4% indicates meaningful learning above chance (25%) but substantial room for improvement.
Model evaluation was performed on a single dataset; external validation is needed.
The system must not be used for clinical diagnosis.
futureWork: Increase the number of emotion classes
Reassess auxiliary tasks from ethical and scientific perspectives
Select the best model directly with emotion-centered criteria
Perform external validation on additional datasets
Run human-centered evaluation studies with expert feedback
Mature the web interface for field-oriented usage scenarios
about.disclaimer: As explicitly stated in the report, this system is not a clinical diagnosis tool; it should be used to support expert evaluation.
